issue_title: 如何注册多个函数分别对应不同的群

issue_body:
I don't know if glad is supposed to work with OSMesa, but currently currently it doesn't. Including `GL/osmesa.h` before glad doesn't work, because `osmesa.h` includes `GL/gl.h`. Reversing the order, it doesn't work because glad doesn't define `GLAPIENTRY` and `osmesa.h` expects it. I've managed to workaround it with
```c++
#include <glad/glad.h>
#define GLAPIENTRY APIENTRY
#include <GL/osmesa.h>
```
but it's not ideal.

Using mesa 18.1.5 and https://glad.dav1d.de/#profile=core&language=c&specification=gl&api=gl%3D3.3 (version 0.1.26) to generate glad header.

issue_title: [Suggestion] Add support to avoid creating dynamic child creation
issue_body:
Hi folks!

I guess would be nice to have a way to avoid creating dynamic child creation. I know this dynamic child creation is here by design, but sometimes this isn't good (for instance, on tests: I would like to ensure I'm using the proper key names on some context)

Current scenario (Again, I know it's dynamic creating by design):
```
from dotmap import DotMap
>>> d = {'key': 'value'}
>>> dm = DotMap(d)
>>> dm.unexisting.key.i.would.like.to.see.burning
DotMap()
```

My suggestion is to add a way to avoid that, for instance:
```
from dotmap import DotMap
>>> d = {'key': 'value'}
>>> dm = DotMap(d, avoid_dynamic_child_creation=True)  # better name please.  =P
>>> dm.unexisting.key.i.would.like.to.see.burning
KeyError: 'unexisting'
```

Thanks!

issue_title: addict outputs {'__unicode__': {}} rather than empty string when used in django templates.
issue_body:
Here is a rough idea of the code I'm running:

    from addict import Dict
    def get_dictionary(self, orig_dict):
        new_dict = Dict({'default_key':'default_value'})
        if(orig_dict["code"]==0):
            new_dict.other_key = "Double Quoted String"
        elif(orig_dict["code"]==1):
            new_dict.other_key = "Different Double Quoted String"
        return new_dict

and eventually when I use it with Django's `render_to_string("xml_template.xml",dictionary=context_dictionary)` into this template:

    <ParentTag>
        <MyFirstTag>{{new_dict.default_key}}</MyFirstTag>
        <MySecondTag>{{new_dict.other_key}}</MySecondTag>
    </ParentTag>

And it renders as:

    <ParentTag>
        <MyFirstTag>default_value</MyFirstTag>
        <MySecondTag>{'__unicode__': {}</MySecondTag>
    </ParentTag>


I think that (for some reason) addict is returning an empty dictionary, but the output is being mangled. I know the docs say you *can* use `to_dict()` but it implies you don't need to.

Any idea why this happens?

issue_title: Unable to build i386 binary with XCode 4.0
issue_body:
keyring 0.6.2 setup.py has a section to empty ARCHFLAGS environment variable if it detects XCode 4.0. This is preventing building i386 binaries when you have XCode 4.0 installed, because you need to set ARCHFLAGS="-arch i386".

If you build with above flags and do lipo -info /path/to/keyring.so, it should show i386 binary. On Lion with XCode 4.0 I see x86_64.

I believe you should modify the setup code to remove all "-arch ppc\S*" instructions from ARCHFLAGS, not just clear it completely. 

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/53
- Originally reported by: [heikkitoivonen](http://bitbucket.org/heikkitoivonen)
- Originally created at: 2011-12-08T19:42:17.440

issue_title: Provided payload hash should be used when validating MAC
issue_body:
It appears that mohawk calculates [1] the content hash even when a hash was provided in the request, and uses that calculated hash for validating the MAC [2] .that the content hash is created by mohawk and used for validating the MAC when according to a statement [3,4] in the node hawk implementation the hash provided in the request should be used.

One of the issues that comes up when not calculating using the provided hash is that if there are encoding differences with the payload, this will result in a different payload hash.  However, the error that's returned is a mac mismatch when really the useful message about where to look is with a content mismatch, such as the one raised later on in base.py with the MisComputedContentHash exception.

One way to duplicate this is to use a different client for sending a hawk request that doesn't automatically encode the payload for you (such as the node hawk client).  mohawk utf8 encodes the payload and calculates the hash while the node client allows you to use whatever encoding you wish.

[1] https://github.com/kumar303/mohawk/blob/master/mohawk/base.py#L42
[2] https://github.com/kumar303/mohawk/blob/master/mohawk/base.py#L48
[3] "If the payload is available at the time of authentication, the server uses the hash value provided by the client to construct the normalized string and validates the MAC"
[4] https://github.com/hueniverse/hawk/blob/master/README.md#payload-validation

issue_title: Missing completions in "from X import A, B" form
issue_body:
Hello!
For the form `from X import `, Jedi gives correct completions for items in `X`, but for the form `from X import A, `, Jedi offers completions only for global names.

Example:

```Python
import jedi

s1 = jedi.Script("from itertools import ", line=1, column=22)
s2 = jedi.Script("from itertools import chain, ", line=1, column=29)
c1 = s1.completions()
c2 = s2.completions()
```

Here, `c1` contains `chain`, `combinations`, etc., but `c2` contains global `abs`, `all`, etc. I would expect both `c1` and `c2` to be equal.

(Originally reported as jorgenschaefer/elpy/#732)

issue_title: Add process_iter(attrs, ad_value) parameters to retrieve multiple info in one shot
issue_body:
Right now the designated way to iterate over all process and query them is:

```
>>> import psutil
>>> for proc in psutil.process_iter():
...     try:
...         pinfo = proc.as_dict(attrs=['pid', 'name'])
...     except psutil.NoSuchProcess:
...         pass
...     else:
...         print(pinfo)
...
{'pid': 1, 'name': 'systemd'}
{'pid': 2, 'name': 'kthreadd'}
{'pid': 3, 'name': 'ksoftirqd/0'}
...
```

This is a bit verbose though and requires a try/except. Also it's a bit error prone (race condition) as the user may forget to catch NoSuchProcess. 
Proposal is to introduce 2 new parameters, `attrs` and `ad_value`, which will be passed to `Process.as_dict()`. 
When `attrs` is specified `Process.as_dict()` is called and the resulting dict is stored as a `info` attribute which is attached to the returned `Process`  instance:

```
>>> import psutil
>>> for proc in psutil.process_iter(attrs=['pid', 'name']):
...     print(proc.info)
...
{'pid': 1, 'name': 'systemd'}
{'pid': 2, 'name': 'kthreadd'}
{'pid': 3, 'name': 'ksoftirqd/0'}
```

This also introduces the possibility to use list/dict comprehensions:

```
>>> import psutil
>>> info = [p.info for p in psutil.process_iter(attrs=['pid', 'name'])]
>>> info
[{'pid': 1, 'name': 'systemd'}, {'pid': 2, 'name': 'kthreadd'}, {'pid': 3, 'name': 'ksoftirqd/0'}, ...
```

Use-case: we are implementing CNI driver [1] for Kubernetes and we are following CNI's [2] approach where the VETH pair is initially created inside the container and one end is then moved to the host namespace. issue_title: Performance comparison for asn1 decode of SNMP
issue_body:
This is not really an issue - it's more by way of informational/comparison, given the focus on performance.

We use the ancient https://github.com/jpwarren/libsnmp for a local/bespoke high-throughput SNMP poller. I've recently tried using pysnmp had lower performance, apparently due to pyasn1 - see https://github.com/etingof/pysnmp/issues/44

I recently came across asn1crypto and decided to try and build a simple SNMP encoder/decoder and compare performance. It seems that asn1crypto is faster than pysnmp/pyasn1 but still slower than the libsnmp code.

This is in many ways an unfair comparison as libsnmp is a very constrained application and can avoid being completely general - but I thought it was worth raising here in case it reveals any opportunities for optimisation.

The test script I use can be found here:

https://gist.github.com/philmayers/67b9300d8fb7282481a1a6af5ed45818

Some quick results from my box

cpython2.7

```
timeit for libsnmp 1000 iterations per-call 0.366773ms
timeit for pysnmp 1000 iterations per-call 1.913801ms
timeit for asn1crypto 1000 iterations per-call 0.784892ms
```

pypy 5.4.0 (much larger iteraction count to let JIT kick in)

```
timeit for libsnmp 100000 iterations per-call 0.026978ms
timeit for pysnmp 100000 iterations per-call 0.192198ms
timeit for asn1crypto 100000 iterations per-call 0.066650ms
```

Feel free to close this if the info is not of interest - as noted, comparing a very constrained libsnmp to the much more general encoding in asn1crypto is possibly not reasonable/useful.

Thanks for your work on the project - the code is great quality and I really like the API
It would be nice to add keybinding customization possibility to all commands/functions.  I think it should also allow multiple keys per function.

issue_title: Year-match isn't treated equally among providers
issue_body:
Hey,

When comparing the match determination of OpenSubtitles and all the other providers, there seems to be a huge difference: OpenSubtitles provider only adds the year match to the matches list [if the video had a year (bool(video.year)) and both the video and the subtitle have the same year set](https://github.com/Diaoul/subliminal/blob/develop/subliminal/providers/opensubtitles.py#L76).

In comparison to that, [let's say Addic7ed](https://github.com/Diaoul/subliminal/blob/develop/subliminal/providers/addic7ed.py#L55): It blindly compares the value of `video.year` with the value of `subtitle.year` - which adds `year` to the matches list, if both values are None.

If I'm not wrong, this results in a pretty distinct behaviour: All other providers besides OpenSubtitles match 44 points too high - or OpenSubtitles generally matches 44 points too low, if the year isn't set on the video.

I'd expect consistent behaviour, and I think the OpenSubtitles provider is wrong, because a None-year should match if both the video and the subtitles have it.

issue_title: MongoDB 3.3+ only supports CSRS for mongos --configdb parameter
issue_body:
For MongoDB 3.3+ `mlaunch` should default to CSRS; SCCC is no longer supported.

Currently mlaunch defaults to SCCC and `mongos` will fail to start up:

```
$ mlaunch init --sharded 1 --replica
launching: mongod on port 27018
launching: mongod on port 27019
launching: mongod on port 27020
launching: config server on port 27021
replica set 'shard01' initialized.
launching: mongos on port 27017
can't start process, return code 2.
```

The `mongos` error is:

> BadValue: configdb supports only replica set connection string

- Start a section in the documentation for developers
- The objective here is to give developers enough clarity to contribute the code of eppy.
- Contributors to eppy are likely to be not-professional programmers, and this documentation has to be tuned for such contributors.

issue_title: Hmmm, GPL... possible to change to BSD2 please?
issue_body:
Hi,

GPL is quite restrictive.  My own libraries are BSD2, not compatiable.  Please can you consider relicensing as BSD2?

Regression in v1.6, probably due to missing `angular.copy()`.
See also https://discuss.mopidy.com/t/mopidy-mobile-error-creating-playlists/1012.

So right now if you want to make your py3status modules configurable you have to handle everything in the module itself. I think it would be really nice if py3status would provide some kind of framework for that. 
It could expose some default settings to each module (e.g. the title) and if the module needs more settings it might extend the configuration. 
Maybe it could also be used to control which modules should actually be used instead of just loading everything in the py3status folder.

I think the format should be mirroring the format used for i3status.conf. It would probably make sense to place the config somewhere in either the .i3 or the py3status folder.

What are your thoughts on this?

issue_title: Add static typing to Hypothesis API
issue_body:
The new typing module in 3.5 and [PEP 0484](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0484/) add static type annotations to Hypothesis. It would be nice for the API to support this, as the public API is actually pretty static in nature. stub files offer a compatibility mode, so this can be done transparently without dropping support for Python 2 (which is sadly there for the long haul).

Steps this would require:

1. Making Strategy a generic type with a single parameter, Strategy[T] that specifies the type of value generated. Note that Strategy is covariant in its type parameter.
2. Providing static types for all of the public API in hypothesis and hypothesis.strategies.
3. Running a mypy checker on Hypothesis + its test suite as part of the build

Things to note:

1. ```given``` and ```builds``` cannot be precisely typed with this type system, but they can still have types that are better than nothing by constraining their \*args and \*\*kwargs to have a strategy type (this is actually quite useful because it will catch two common gotchas when using this).
2. Settings is going to be quite hard to type correctly and is probably not worth bothering with.
3. I am *not* proposing to make Hypothesis internals typed. In particular templates for strategies will remain completely untyped. I've worked out what typing this accurately requires in the past and PEP 0484 can't begin to express it.

At the moment, the version of systemd-python-234.tar.gz is returned as None, with the 234 being currently included in the package name. However according to PEP-0440, having an integer version, while being a bit unusual, is still valid (https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/#id22), and passes is_canonical here: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/#id79

I've raised an issue for this to discuss, as there is at least one existing case used in test_guess_name_version_from_filename which will be invalidated (ocflib-3-4.tar.gz, [here](https://github.com/chriskuehl/dumb-pypi/blob/master/tests/main_test.py#L21)) if we changed this, so wanted to make sure that we are in agreement before I make a PR.

Relevant package for this case in pypi.org (systemd-python) is [here](https://pypi.org/project/systemd-python/).

Hi,

thanks for providing this useful library. I'm using it on a Raspberry PI to plot temperature data over a webpage. However, after upgrading to version 0.1.2 using pip the library does not work and my small app logs messages like this

I figured out that this problem could be related to a change in rrdtoolmodule.c in line 431 (see https://github.com/commx/python-rrdtool/blob/master/rrdtoolmodule.c#L431).

Is there a way to be able to upgrade to version 0.1.2 while having the possibility to use the graph function?

Best regards and thanks in advance

issue_body:
Windows 7 64-bit
pyexecjs 1.1.0
pyv8 1.0
NodeJS 4.0.0

Using execjs with pyv8 (it seems that PyV8 takes precedence):

```Python
import execjs

execjs.get().name
Out[2]: u'PyV8'

from react import jsx
jsx.transform('helloworld.jsx')
Traceback (most recent call last):

  File "<ipython-input-4-ba80c49e788e>", line 1, in <module>
    jsx.transform('helloworld.jsx')

  File "C:\Python27\python-2.7.6.amd64\lib\site-packages\react\jsx.py", line 64, in transform
    return JSXTransformer().transform(jsx_path, **opts)

  File "C:\Python27\python-2.7.6.amd64\lib\site-packages\react\jsx.py", line 51, in transform
    js = self.transform_string(i.read(), **kwargs)

  File "C:\Python27\python-2.7.6.amd64\lib\site-packages\react\jsx.py", line 45, in transform_string
    raise TransformError(e.message[7:])

TransformError: ()
```

'JScript' was my original runtime:

```Python
import os
os.environ['EXECJS_RUNTIME'] = 'JScript'
jsx.transform('helloworld.jsx')
Traceback (most recent call last):

  File "<ipython-input-7-ba80c49e788e>", line 1, in <module>
    jsx.transform(helloworld.jsx')

  File "C:\Python27\python-2.7.6.amd64\lib\site-packages\react\jsx.py", line 64, in transform
    return JSXTransformer().transform(jsx_path, **opts)

  File "C:\Python27\python-2.7.6.amd64\lib\site-packages\react\jsx.py", line 51, in transform
    js = self.transform_string(i.read(), **kwargs)

  File "C:\Python27\python-2.7.6.amd64\lib\site-packages\react\jsx.py", line 45, in transform_string
    raise TransformError(e.message[7:])

TransformError: or: 'undefined' is null or not an object
```

But installing NodeJS and using it as the runtime works:

```Python
os.environ['EXECJS_RUNTIME'] = 'Node'

In [9]: jsx.transform('helloworld.jsx')
Out[9]: u'var HelloMessage = React.createClass({displayName: \'HelloMessage\',\n  render: function() {\n    return React.createElement("div", null, "Hello ", this.props.name);\n  }\n});\n\nReact.render(React.createElement(HelloMessage, {name: "John"}), mountNode);'
```

So I guess that should be added to the readme. I have uninstalled PyV8 and now Node is the default runtime under execjs.
Not even fancy ones, just something that runs at least a happy path with a small amount of text to make sure everything is valid.

issue_title: support for spaces in list of hosts

create regexp to match either single host or list of hosts

if sys.version_info > (3, 0):

issue_title: Allow modification of parsing grammar at runtime, without reloading the entire grammar
issue_body:
Imagine if you would, a whole sea of users naively trying to improve their forks of a base grammar all on the same server where each grammar runs in a mostly idle state on occasional sporadic user-generated input. The more users that have to reload the entire grammar they've been working on, the more the server might bog down. It would be considerably better if only the changes to the grammars needed to be loaded while the grammars worked. Is this at all doable?

Also, on the general subject of extensibility, rules are going to have to be added to the head of the grammar, the tail of the grammar, but also inserted into precise places in the middle of the grammar, because PEG requires prioritized order like that; probably by referencing a rule above or below which to insert a new rule.

Might I suggest something like:
grammar=Grammar ("""
    a="A"
    d="D"
    """)
grammar.insert ("""b="B"""" below "a")
grammar.insert ("""c="C"""" above "d")

Also, to delete a rule:
grammar.delete ("d")

And, so grammar editors can know what the currently running grammar looks like:
grammar.print ("optional_rule")

issue_title: patch_data should work with empty arrays
issue_body:
Patch_data ignores empty arrays. This becomes issue when trying to remove all elements from specific model, like in the example, remove all items from order. I think patch_data should be in this situation `{"items": []"]`, but it's actually just an empty dictionary.

```
$ python example.py 
wtforms version: 2.0.1
wtforms_json version: 0.2.10
form.data {'items': [{'name': 'Foo'}, {'name': 'Bar'}]}
form.patch_data {}
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "example.py", line 67, in <module>
    assert(form.patch_data == {"items": []})
AssertionError
```

Here is working example to reproduce the issue:

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
from sqlalchemy import Column, ForeignKey, Integer, Unicode
from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
import wtforms
from wtforms import Form
from wtforms.fields import (
    FormField,
    StringField,
    FieldList,
)
import wtforms_json  # noqa
wtforms_json.init()

Base = declarative_base()

import pprint
print "wtforms version:", wtforms.__version__
print "wtforms_json version:", wtforms_json.__version__


# 1. Setup SQLAlchemy Models

class Item(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'item'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    order_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey("order.id"))
    order = relationship("Order", backref="items")
    name = Column(Unicode())


class Order(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'order'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)


instance = Order(items=[
    Item(name="Foo"),
    Item(name="Bar"),
])


# 2. Forms definition

class ItemForm(Form):
    name = StringField()


class OrderForm(Form):
    items = FieldList(FormField(ItemForm))


# 3. Create form instance

json_data = {
    "items": []
}

form = OrderForm.from_json(json_data, obj=instance)

print u"form.data", pprint.pformat(form.data)
print u"form.patch_data", pprint.pformat(form.patch_data)


# 4. patch_data should be empty list

assert(form.patch_data == {"items": []})

```

Is there anything that can be done to resolve this issue?

We want a CLI option to handle refreshing Trusted Advisor limit checks. This will require and additional IAM permission.

Hello guys,

 I thinks there's an issue on the way depth socket messages are handled by the DepthCacheManager. Playing around with it for several days and storing the values in a time series database, I noticed after some restarts that sometimes, either the best bid or best ask would not move until the next restart. I believe it's due to 2 things: 

1. 

The start_depth_socket() method of the BinanceSocketManager listens by default to the [Diff.Depth Stream endpoint](https://github.com/binance-exchange/binance-official-api-docs/blob/master/web-socket-streams.md#diff-depth-stream) and not to the [Depth Websocket EndPoint](https://www.binance.com/restapipub.html#depth-wss-endpoint) described on the api doc of Binance website. The difference between those 2 is that the diff depth stream endpoint provides a first_update_id and a last_update_id ('U' and 'u' in the json response). The DepthCacheManager does not handle this first_update_id as described [here](https://github.com/binance-exchange/binance-official-api-docs/blob/master/web-socket-streams.md#how-to-manage-a-local-order-book-correctly)

2. 

From the constructor of the DepthCacheManager: 
`self._start_socket()`
`self._init_cache()`

==> what can happen is that the self._start_socket() method takes longer than the self._init_cache(), in that case, the sate of the order book would not be consistent between the one returned by the rest api call and the one returned by the websocket. For example, _init_cache() could return a LastUpdateId = 1, and _start_socket() could return a first message with 'U' = 3 and 'u' = 4, which would mean we did not process the update 2.
So I think it is necessary to add code to wait for the websocket to have received at least 1 message before starting the rest call to get the state of the current order book, and also start processing websocket from U == LastUpdateId +1 (and not U <= LastUpdateId +1).

Also, another weird stuff I noticed (this time related directly to binance websocket api), is receiving messages like this when listening to the diff depth stream: 

{
  "e": "depthUpdate", // Event type
  "E": 123456789,     // Event time
  "s": "BNBBTC",      // Symbol
  "U": 157,           // First update ID in event
  "u": 160,           // Final update ID in event
  "b": [              // Bids to be updated
    [
      "0.0024",       // price level to be updated
      "10",
      []              // ignore
    ]
  ],
  "a": [              // Asks to be updated
    [
      "0.0026",       // price level to be updated
      "100",          // quantity
      []              // ignore
    ]
  ]
}
==> if 'U' = 157 and 'u' = 160, I am assuming I should see 4 update messages. But here, only 1 bid and 1 ask have been updated. Am I wrong in my assumption? (this json is from [binance official doc](https://github.com/binance-exchange/binance-official-api-docs/blob/master/web-socket-streams.md#diff-depth-stream) but I've observed similar messages myself).

issue_title: "notify" function does not work with server running C++ JSON RPC
issue_body:
I am currently trying to interface with a C++ JSON RPC server (https://github.com/cinemast/libjson-rpc-cpp) over http and found that the notify function does not work. However, I believe I have found the problem.

 I am not sure if the interpretation of "Notification" in JSON 2.0 is up for debate (http://www.jsonrpc.org/specification#notification) but it seems while this python library simply puts **None** for the "id" member in the request_dict, the C++ library is expecting there to be no "id" item in the payload at all.

I got around this problem by editing _lines 294 and 299 in 'http.py'_ with **del request_dict["id"]** instead. I'm not sure if this is really the right fix for this problem and I have not tested to see if this would work with the server running on python.

At the moment confirmation token can be logs user in automatically.
I suggest this behavior should be, so this can be configured as required.

Other issue with the current confirmation tokens is they can be reused to login without using password as long as that token is not expired.

issue_title: Add support for CouchDB Admin Party mode
issue_body: Currently you must have security set up in CouchDB in order to use the lib with CouchDB.  Since often CouchDB is set up with "Admin Party" privileges it would be good for the lib to support that as well.

issue_title: Memory exceed issue after the first round
issue_body:
Currently I am working on a huge data set. The thing is that if I just put the whole training data in mx.Create.Feedforward, the memory will soon be filled up and fail to run. A easy example is like that.
`data <- mx.symbol.Variable("data")`
`fc1 <- mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data, num_hidden = 3000)`
`fc2 <- mx.symbol.FullyConnected(fc1, num_hidden = 10000)`
`out <- mx.symbol.LinearRegressionOutput(data = fc2)`
`net.dl <- mx.model.FeedForward.create(
  X = input,
  y = truth,
  symbol = out,
  ctx = mx.cpu(),
  num.round = 10,
  learning.rate = 0.001,
  momentum = 0.9,
  eval.metric = mx.metric.rmse,
  array.batch.size = 10,
  array.layout = "colmajor"
)`
The weird thing is that it can run the first round and get the rmse result like:
[1] Train-rmse=2.81730335008859
Then the memory filled up and my job was killed. Why crush after the first round? Will the memory requirement become larger in the deeper round?  Actually I am working on a 512GB mem compute node on the cluster, my data is about 6GB (10000*100000 matrix). I suppose it won't take so much memory.(If I change the hidden nodes to 300 it's ok but that's not the thing I want)

issue_title: 8 gpu no speed up, delay caused by main thread
issue_body:
Hi all,

I am running resnet backbone on 4 gpus, and when we try to use all 8, it provides no speed-up. We have pinned down the problem to the main thread, specifically, the training loop. I understand that all computations and real forward are done on GPUs asynchronously, but still the main thread need to call the python commands like self.conv1(data) to start building computation graphs and perhaps allocate some resources. I have confirmed this as the speed bottleneck. What confuses me is that when I called hybridize, 8 gpus is still only 20% faster than 4 gpus. Why is hybrid_forward so time consuming? How can I bypass this problem and really utilize my nuclear weapons?

issue_title: Shared memory leak when using mxnet.gluon.data.DataLoader
issue_body:
## Description
I found there may exists shared memory leak in mxnet.gluon.data.DataLoader

## Environment info (Required)
Python 2.7.13 , Anaconda
Nvidia-docker Cuda-8.0
mxnet-1.0(installed from pip)


## Minimum reproducible example
```
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet.gluon.data import Dataset
from mxnet import nd
from mxnet.gluon.data import DataLoader
import numpy as np
class TestIter(Dataset):
    def __init__(self):
        pass
    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        return mx.nd.array(np.zeros(shape = (1,3,1024,1024))), \
            mx.nd.array(np.zeros(shape = (1,3,1024,1024)))
    def __len__(self):
        return 100
def batch_fn(data):
    imgs = []
    heatmaps = []
    for sample in data:
        img,heatmap = sample
        imgs.append(mx.nd.expand_dims(img,axis = 0))
        heatmaps.append(mx.nd.expand_dims(heatmap,axis = 0))
    data = [mx.nd.concatenate(imgs,axis = 0)]
    label = [mx.nd.concatenate(heatmaps,axis = 0)]
    return mx.io.DataBatch(data = data,label = label)    
if __name__ == "__main__":
    data_loader = DataLoader(TestIter(),batch_size = 2,shuffle = True,batchify_fn = batch_fn,num_workers = 8)
    for _ in range(1000):
        for batch in data_loader:
            d = batch.data[0].asnumpy()
            l = batch.label[0].asnumpy()
            print(d.shape,l.shape)
```

## Steps to reproduce

This code dead after some time, I found the reason is shared memory usage is increasing when running this code, but if I set num_works to 0, it will be ok. I think this is a bug.

I take a try the mxnet.gluon.data.vision.transforms, the code is following

`

test_data = gluon.data.vision.MNIST(train=False, transform=transforms.ToTensor())

for epoch in range(2):

`

but it raise following error:

issue_title: Management command revamp for argparse broken commands
issue_body:
`runserver_plus` no longer accepts the positional argument of `0.0.0.0:8000` (ip address/port combo) because the changes in this pull request ignore positional arguments. It looks like `optparse` was forgiving with regards to positional arguments, just passing them through to be handled. `argparse` expects them to be defined, it seems.  This is directly related to #820. The PR that causes this (mis)behaviour is #804.

See Django `master`'s current runserver code for this positional argument: https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/core/management/commands/runserver.py#L39

This is the error I see when using django-extensions `master` branch:

```
(myproject)vagrant@vagrant-ubuntu-trusty-64:/vagrant$ python manage.py runserver_plus 0.0.0.0:8000
usage: manage.py runserver_plus [-h] [--version] [-v {0,1,2,3}]
                                [--settings SETTINGS]
                                [--pythonpath PYTHONPATH] [--traceback]
                                [--no-color] [--ipv6] [--noreload] [--browser]
                                [--adminmedia ADMIN_MEDIA_PATH]
                                [--nothreading] [--threaded]
                                [--output OUTPUT_FILE] [--print-sql]
                                [--cert CERT_PATH] [--extra-file EXTRA_FILES]
                                [--reloader-interval RELOADER_INTERVAL]
                                [--pdb] [--ipdb] [--pm]
                                [--startup-messages STARTUP_MESSAGES]
                                [--nostatic] [--insecure]
manage.py runserver_plus: error: unrecognized arguments: 0.0.0.0:8000
```

I suspect that other django-extensions' management commands will need a positional argument review as well - particularly when you see other related issues like #820.

issue_title: Do we need LDAP support patches?
issue_body:
We support LDAP Multiplugins and patching therefore PAS. Do we really want to support this in PlonePAS? 
In my opinion this must be moved to PloneLDAP. More modern PAS implementations such as pas.plugins.ldap doesnt need this patches.

Old (deprecated?) `@@manage-portlets` provided quicker editing capabilities since user could see/compare/edit at least two portlet managers at once.

Plone 5 alpha introduced `@@topbar-manage-portlets` as a modal which was then changed to a normal page because 2nd-level overlays didn't work well. I'm not sure if shouldn't have just returned to `@@manage-portlets` but since we didn't I suggest we add a jumplist widget to make managing portlets a more pleasurable experience. Currently after you enter one of the`@@topbar-manage-portlets` pages you have to click `#portal-logo` and use the toolbar again to manage another column. Also too bad if you weren't in homepage; you'll have no help to navigate back to where you were.
Being a core package it makes sense to unify the translations in a single source.

issue_title: RegistryReader has bad performance on sites with a lot of users
issue_labels: [{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/plone/plone.app.querystring/labels/01%20type:%20bug', 'name': '01 type: bug', 'color': 'fa0000'}, {'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/plone/plone.app.querystring/labels/31%20needs:%20help', 'name': '31 needs: help', 'color': '00fafa'}, {'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/plone/plone.app.querystring/labels/43%20lvl:%20complex', 'name': '43 lvl: complex', 'color': '274e13'}, {'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/plone/plone.app.querystring/labels/99%20tag:%20Plone%205.x', 'name': '99 tag: Plone 5.x', 'color': 'f3f3f3'}]
issue_body:
Collections call view ``@@qsOptions`` for the query field options. That view is defined in ``plone.app.content``, but it delegates all work to **QuerystringRegistryReader** in ``plone.app.querystring``. Registry reader "simply" dumps configuration for all queryable fields and operations (both enabled and disabled), including **all** distinct values for related vocabularies. For sites with a lot of editors, vocabulary for **Creator** field may become large (and all values are twice in the resulting JSON, once for all indexes and once for sortable indexes). Yet, if the sites is using LDAP (tested only with LDAPUserFolder), rendering ``@@qsOptions`` requires fetching full name for all users, who have created content in the site. With some bad luck with the current LDAPUserFolder caches, this may choke a single instance site for a while.

I don't see any obvious fix for this. Comments and ideas are welcome.

```python
import re, zmq

issue_title: leave non encryptable values in cleartext
issue_body:
Booleans, for example, cannot be encrypted. We could store them as a string type and restore the original type on decrypt, but there's little value in encrypted something that can only be true or false.
Instead, `sops` should just leave non-encryptable types in clear, and issue a warning to the user that the file contains values that won't be stored securely.

We could still include those values in the MAC computation, I think...

A requested feature for a long time. Finally with the help of Treeherder we can get this implemented. It's not that easy but with a couple of API requests it can be done. So here an example for one of the latest beta-releases which happened to be:

https://treeherder.mozilla.org/#/jobs?repo=mozilla-beta&revision=5c042dab34a2

Steps to do:

* Get the resultset for the given revision: https://treeherder.mozilla.org/api/project/mozilla-beta/resultset/?revision=5c042dab34a257de85b7eaf2bc81aaad16b8a1b0

* Retrieve the option collection hash to use: https://treeherder.mozilla.org/api/optioncollectionhash/

* With the `results[0]['id']` (2441562) found you can query for all the jobs run for this particular changeset. What we want is to apply filters to reduce the time until we got all results. Keep in mind that we need a hash for Treeherder supported platforms and similar: https://treeherder.mozilla.org/api/project/mozilla-beta/jobs/?result_set_id=2441562&count=2000&exclusion_profile=false&job_type_name=Build&platform=linux32&option_collection_hash=102210fe594ee9b33d82058545b1ed14f4c8206e 

* With the `results[0]['id']` (737977) found you can query for the artifacts of the job: https://treeherder.mozilla.org/api/project/mozilla-beta/artifact/?job_id=737977&name=Job+Info&type=json

* We can use the log URL strip of the filename and take that as base path for all the files contained in this folder.

issue_title: Unable to cancel s3 object download
issue_body:
The following script shows what we are trying to do (set a timeout for a download and cancel if it exceeds that time):

```python
import signal
import sys
from boto3.s3.transfer import TransferConfig
import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)

import botoutil.core  # small wrapper around getting credentials
boto3_session = botoutil.core.boto3_session()  # creating a standard session object with custom auth credentials

s3_resource = boto3_session.resource('s3')

obj = s3_resource.Object('some_bucket', 'some_fairly_large_file')
max_secs = 1
local_path = '/mnt/tmp/big_zero'
config = TransferConfig({'num_download_attempts': 1})


class TimeoutError(Exception): pass


def handler(signum, frame):
    print "Got timeout error"
    raise TimeoutError()

signal.signal(signal.SIGALRM, handler)
try:
    obj.download_file(local_path, Config=config)
    print True
except TimeoutError:
    print False
finally:
    signal.signal(signal.SIGALRM, signal.SIG_DFL)
    signal.alarm(0)

sys.exit()
```

boto3==1.3.1 works fine with this script (stopping execution after 1 second), but boto3==1.4.0 insists on continuing to download the file until it is done (according to the DEBUG logs being printed).

We tried updating to the latest s3transfer/botocore/boto3 versions [botocore=1.4.58; s3transfer==0.1.5].

The problem appears to be related to the download running in a thread. It would be quite nice if there was a way to tell boto3 to stop a specified download once it has started, or, alternatively, turn off the use of threads for downloading altogether as an option. We'd like to not be stuck on boto3==1.3.1 forever.

Unlike "conventional" zipapps, it seems that shiv adds bootstrap code to unpack the archive and run it from an unpacked directory. This allows it to support compiled extension modules, but it also means that the unpacked archives get left around (at least on Windows, as you can't delete a loaded DLL in Windows, and I believe the code doesn't even try to tidy the unpacked archive up anyway).

I think this behaviour should be documented, at least in the "gotchas" section of the readme, as it's likely to be unexpected for at least some users (and may even be a privacy concern for some).

It would be nice if the tool only unpacked when needed, and warned when building an archive that needed unpacking. But I doubt that's possible in practice (it's essentially the "zip safe" flag that setuptools tried to implement for eggs, that is generally considered to have failed).

issue_title: Remote URL cannot be accessed for git < 2.7 if URL is git@github but SSH keys are not setup
issue_body:
If the system git is < 2.7, and the URL is one that git expects to use SSH access on, but SSH keys are not setup, then `self.repo.git.remote("show", self.name)` [here](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/master/git/remote.py#L539) in remote.py will fail with: 

```python
GitCommandError: Cmd('git') failed due to: exit code(128)
  cmdline: git remote show origin
  stderr: 'Permission denied (publickey).
fatal: Could not read from remote repository.

Please make sure you have the correct access rights
and the repository exists.'
```

This can be fixed by falling back to `git config --get remote.%s.url' % self.name` which just reads the URL directly from the configuration.

issue_title: submodule: Spooky error message during specific submodule operations
issue_body:
Hey all,
I get the error `AttributeError: Cannot retrieve the name of a submodule if it was not set initially` if I perform one of the following tasks on a submodule instance:

* submodule.name
* submodule.url
* submodule.update()
* I think some more...

I create a submodule as followed:
```python
submodule = self.repo.create_submodule(sm_name, sm_path, url=sm_url)
```
And the creation and initial pull works pretty well.
However, if i try to get the configured url:
```python
used_url = submodule.url()
# or submodule.update()
...
  File "/home/daniel/workspace/envs/bmw/lib/python3.5/site-packages/git/objects/submodule/base.py", line 492, in update
    % (remote, self.name))
  File "/home/daniel/workspace/envs/bmw/lib/python3.5/site-packages/gitdb/util.py", line 239, in __getattr__
    return object.__getattribute__(self, attr)
  File "/home/daniel/workspace/envs/bmw/lib/python3.5/site-packages/git/objects/submodule/base.py", line 1135, in name
    return self._name
  File "/home/daniel/workspace/envs/bmw/lib/python3.5/site-packages/gitdb/util.py", line 237, in __getattr__
    self._set_cache_(attr)
  File "/home/daniel/workspace/envs/bmw/lib/python3.5/site-packages/git/objects/submodule/base.py", line 135, in _set_cache_
    raise AttributeError("Cannot retrieve the name of a submodule if it was not set initially")
AttributeError: Cannot retrieve the name of a submodule if it was not set initially
```
For my understanding an initial name is set during my submodule creation.

Any ideas how to avoid this?

OAuth is very inconvenient for certain cases. For example, I have a 5-line Python script in my /bin/ directory that I can use to upload an image from the command line quickly. However, since I need to go through the whole OAuth flow to upload as myself, I need to be visiting the website every single time I want to upload an image, or upload the image without having it go to my page, or take care to store the state somewhere.

All of these options are very onerous, all I want to do is go to my settings page, find an API key and paste it in the script and have it work forever. Is there a way to do that?

The Selenium2Library documentation when describing the look-up strategies states "More complex table look-up strategies are also supported:
Strategy Example Description
css Table Should Contain | css=table.my_class | text Matches by @id or @name attribute
xpath Table Should Contain | xpath=//table/[@name="my_table"] | text Matches by @id or @name attribute
To me it looks as if the **Description** column is incorrect. Looking at the **Strategy** and the **Example** as well as the description for the element look-up strategies. I suspect that the CSS strategy should have a description of "Matches by CSS selector" and xpath - "Matches with an arbitrary XPath expression".

issue_title: Installer should support installing from requirements.txt file
Higher and lower level components is confusing
issue_body:
Hi @virtuald and @PeterJohnson,
I think it's confusing that there are higher and lower level components. I like the way that @frc1418 had `components` and `automations`. I think updating this in the docs would make it easier for beginners to understand the difference, and it would be good for them to live in separate folders.
Let's avoid reinventing the wheel.

issue_title: Capture last install/synced Pipfile.lock hash in virtualenv to skip redundant installs/syncs
issue_body:
I'm always frustrated when install/syncs take significant amounts of time when the lockfile hasn't changed. It'd be handy to have this handled automatically.

We can capture last installed/synced Pipfile.lock hash (meta/hash or similar) in virtualenv directory to skip redundant installs/syncs.

Command line parameters, redirecting to other files, and makefile/other infra changes are more time intensive workarounds that require pipenv introspection. Capturing hashes per venv would be a handy universal solution.

issue_title: Sub-dependencies install, even if main dependencies ignored by markers
issue_body:
If you setup a dependency with `markers`, the dependency itself honors the `markers`, but its sub-dependencies install anyway. In principle, this probably shouldn't happen, but it's compounded into a real issue when the sub-dependencies themselves fail for reasons stated in the `markers`.

------------

##### Expected result
The sub-dependencies of a dependency should be ignored

##### Actual result
Sub-dependencies are (attempted to be) installed, but (seemingly) only for versions <= 3.3

When possible, provide the verbose output (`--verbose`), especially for locking and dependencies resolving issues.

##### Steps to replicate
Please see minimum-reproducible code at [this repo](https://github.com/murrple-1/pipenv-issue), with [this Travis-CI](https://travis-ci.org/murrple-1/pipenv-issue). Notice that the Pipfile succeeds for Python 3.4, 3.5, & 3.6, but fails for 2.7 & 3.3.

installed packages from requirements
`pipenv run pip install -r requirements.txt`

issue_title: --prefix doesn't work for OSX Framework Python
issue_body:
If a user provides `--prefix` to setuptools, setuptools adds a site directory for `${prefix}/lib/python{version}/site-packages` on OSX -

https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/6547430bf289c7285498303ea77eef56702c3a95/setuptools/command/easy_install.py#L1360

Problem is, for OSX Framework Python this isn't true.  The `site-packages` wind up being at `${prefix}/lib/python/site-packages` -https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/b505c47044fb5b8f03b9708ee1f210a4c935baf5/Lib/site.py#L267 (which then calls into https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/a6431f2c8cf4783c2fd522b2f6ee04c3c204237f/Lib/sysconfig.py#L75)

This is causing setuptools to fail when installing packages into a prefixed directory.  For example (on OSX 10.11.15, python 2.7 installed via brew)

```
export PYTHONUSERBASE=$(mktemp -d)
export PYTHONPATH="$(python -m site --user-site)/"
wget https://pypi.python.org/packages/1b/4f/e52b81c47396668deb139b628f4ebb499b3cd39fc05382851fa985d0b642/setuptools-24.3.0.tar.gz#md5=55d77ca2b1f783a71e330b0878da29ec
tar xzf setuptools-24.3.0.tar.gz
(cd setuptools* && python setup.py --quiet install --prefix=$PYTHONUSERBASE)
.
.
.
error: bad install directory or PYTHONPATH

You are attempting to install a package to a directory that is not
on PYTHONPATH and which Python does not read ".pth" files from.  The
installation directory you specified (via --install-dir, --prefix, or
the distutils default setting) was:
.
.
.
```

issue_title: Ensure that copied files are writable
issue_body:
`test_always_copy_option` fails because `rmtree` fails.  Since files are copied with `copy2` and not `copyfile` their access mode could be read-only, even for the copying user.  As a result, the current user won't be able to remove the copied files any more.

This is a problem on Guix where `venv/include/python3.4m/modsupport.h` (among others) is copied as a read-only file.

issue_title: task-level recovery might not work if a FireTask changes dirs
issue_body:
See:

https://github.com/hackingmaterials/atomate/issues/217

I already fixed it so that FW_offline.json is loaded from the correct place. But it looks like the checkpoint object always goes to the "launch_dir" (i.e., the *initial* launch dir) to run recovery tasks. However, if one of the tasks in a FireWork changed directories (e.g., task 3), then subsequent tasks (e.g., tasks 4-6) should be run in that changed directory. So if you do task-level rerun at task 4, it should be in that changed directory.

It seems to me that ``_prev_dir`` in the checkpointing dict should not be created by LaunchPad but rather using os.get_cwd() when the rocket does the checkpointing. Or, there should be both ``_prev_dir`` (i.e., launch_dir...) and ``_cur_dir`` (directory of the most recently executing task) in the checkpoint object. When doing task-level recovery, one should use the latter as the directory.

@montoyjh Any thoughts? A lot of this was your code so want to make sure it plays nicely with your knowledge

Most functions of ``scikit-image`` have only one non-keyword argument, which is an input image, and all other arguments are optional keyword arguments. I would suggest that we make the ``sigma`` parameter of ``filters.gaussian`` optional (eg with the default value of 1), for more consistency between functions. In addition, having only one non-kw parameter, which is an image, makes introspection and testing easier.

issue_title: uwsgi segfault, maybe we can tweak the conf to make uwsgi less greedy for travis.

issue_body:
**Problem**

Without changing the JSONSchema you may improve the Elasticsearch mapping especially if you say add computed fields. This will require you to create a new index with the new mapping and reindex all records in the new index. 

Right now, you would have to destroy the existing index, recreate it and reindex. This will mean that you search is down (and possibly other parts of Invenio) until the reindexing has finished would could potentially be quite some time causing service disruption.

**Proposal**

Add features to Invenio-Indexer/Search that allows to create a new index together with the current index. Then take a timestamp T1 and query for all records in a given PID namespace that was modified before this timestamp and send them for reindexing. Once they are all reindexed, you take a new timestamp T2 and reindex any record that was modified in the T1-T2 period. You can redo this until there's no changes (or do a maximum number of iterations). Afterwards you change the alias from old index to the new, and your search should now use the new index and mapping without any downtime.

Issues, index names are currently enforced by invenio-search and you cannot rename an index (AFAIK), i.e. you need something like this (which I don't think invenio-search is capable of today).
- records-record-v1.0.0
- records-record-v1.0.0-2
- https://github.com/inveniosoftware/kwalitee/issues/71
It looks like the registration for is not correctly populated.

It was removed in b95d4c932b4995ce9aefad8bc285bb089bfbe0ba because it was undocumented and untested.

The alternative is `zdaemon kill 12`, which requires me to know the number of the SIGUSR2 signal on my system and is just ugh.

Just a note: Since the actual code in this project is so minimal, I've copied it into Zope itself, as of the unreleased Zope2 4.0a3. One project less to release and update, just because some PyPi classifiers changed ;)

We could make a new five.globalrequest 2.0 release, with the code removed, and requiring Zope2 >= 4.0a3, so other packages can still depend on this project and not get confused.

But honestly, that's not really worth it. If someone uses this and the new Zope version at the same time, it's just two event subscribers being called and both calling ``zope.globalrequest.setRequest/ clearRequest``. Calling those functions twice doesn't hurt.

issue_title: Provide more information in conditional functions like `on_fail`
issue_body:
The current conditional functions have signatures like:
```
def on_fail(self, test_name, begin_time)
```

This has very limited information. For example, if a test fails with multiple errors, which is very common in large e2e tests, the `on_fail` would have no way to know exactly what failed.

We should provide more information to the test in these functions.
We could do this by passing in a copy of the test record for a particular test.

issue_title: MSVC C4551 compiler warnings in platform.h
issue_body:
I was taking a look at updating Firefox' vendored copy of brotli to version 1.0.4 today.

Along the way, I ran into Werror bustage in platform.h within the newly-added `BROTLI_UNUSED_FUNCTION void BrotliSuppressUnusedFunctions(void)` block. MSVC gives a "warning C4551: function call missing argument list" error for each subsequent line within it.

The warning is fixable by explicitly making those all references instead, i.e. `BROTLI_UNUSED(&BrotliSuppressUnusedFunctions)` instead of `BROTLI_UNUSED(BrotliSuppressUnusedFunctions)`. As far as I can tell, everything compiles and functions fine with GCC and clang as well with this change.

issue_title: There is no good way to estimate the size of out when decompressing
issue_body:
I am using BrotliDecompressBufferStreaming which does not provide a way to determine required size for output buffer. For large outputs, I am receiving BROTLI_RESULT_NEEDS_MORE_OUTPUT and the only solution I could work so far is to blindly increase the size of the output buffer and retry.

Possible ways to address these shortcomings.
1. when BROTLI_RESULT_NEEDS_MORE_OUTPUT is returned, available_in would contain the size of the required buffer.
2. Even better solution, that would avoid memory fragmentation, is to allow the caller placing output into chained buffers. I.e. available_in could return the size of the input  buffer processed so far. In this case, one could create new buffer in a loop until available_in does not drop to 0.

As the code is very simple, like this
```
import fire


def test(current):
    print current

    
if __name__ == '__main__':
    fire.Fire(test)
```
if I pass the parameter like this,`python test_fire.py --current=297e7814231`,it print `inf`，but，when I pass the other parameters，it shows good.

why it happend?

issue_title: Add SubUnit counts to the post-election results file
issue_body: See $SUBJECT.
Although hdf5 is certainly the better format it's an additional requirement. For this simple file we might want to just use `csv` instead.

Rather than open a billion issues at once, I'm going to open a single issue here.  We can break out more substantive issues into separate pieces later if we want.

tears.py
-----------
- `create_returns_tearsheet`
  * It's unclear what the `live_start_date` parameter does from reading the docstring.
  * Is it not possible (or not useful?) to use this tearsheet purely on a backtest.  Given that we want people to be able to use this on the Quantopian research platform, which currently only has access to backtest results, it seems important to be able to support pure backtest visualization.
  * Similarly, it's unclear what the `backtest_days_pct` parameter does.  These seem to have the same informational content: why are there two ways of passing this data?
  * Why are there two separate benchmarks?  How are these used?
  * Running this tearsheet with default arguments requires an internet connection because it pulls SPY and IEF from Yahoo.  Can we package these two ETFs in `data` so that this can run offline?
  * Calling `set_plot_defaults` clobbers the user's matplotlib params.  We should use `matplotlib.style.context` instead.
  * We only call `set_plot_defaults` from `create_returns_tearsheet`.  Should we be doing it for the other plots as well?
  
pos.py
---------
Meta commentary: all of the docstrings need to specify how the input data is to be represented.  Telling me that I need to pass in "a dataframe of positions" isn't useful if I don't know how you expect those to be represented.  This is particularly important because some functions expect absolute magnitudes of positions, and other functions expect percentage-based inputs.

- `get_portfolio_alloc`
  - We take three transposes unnecessarily.  These should use `axis=1` instead.
  - What are the expected indices on the input/output frames (I assume index=dates, columns=assets?)
- `get_long_short_pos`
  - What does "the positions that the strategy takes over time" mean?  There are lots of ways that this data could be represented in a dataframe.  
- `extract_pos`
  - This should probably just be called `extract_positions`.

issue_title: move restore back to core libraries
issue_body:
I don't think moving restore.py to an outside script was on the whole a net benefit.  While I understand not wanting to burden the main code with platform-specific dependencies, that puts us in the odd position of having to have a full file path in the config for something which is a critical function of Patroni.  Also, if alienating the WAL-E content was your goal, naming the script "restore.py" wasn't the way to do it.

I suggest instead that we have a core function of patroni called restore, which is pg_basebackup-only restore.  Other restore scripts would be called only if they are defined in config.yml, and only for alternate mechanisms (e.g. wal-e.py, barman.py).

issue_title: Merge barèmes IPP / split param.xml / clean them
issue_body:
This is a description of the work of @benjello and @eraviart, and the discussions of yesterday. 

_Barèmes IPP_ are XLSX files corresponding to sections of the legislation's parameters. Each file has tabs that can be mapped to nodes in param.xml : 

![selection_008](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1177762/12444744/b81b18f8-bf60-11e5-9b3b-0006d189ef45.png)

The idea is to merge these _barèmes_ into OpenFisca's parameters. All of this happens in branch baremes-ipp until #407 is fixed. 
1. IPP is now using a [gitlab repository](https://git.framasoft.org/french-tax-and-benefit-tables/ipp-tax-and-benefit-tables-xlsx) to store these xlsx files and use issues. 
   Writing in tables is easier than handling XML for economists (it is after all a graphical interface to another XML format), but the main drawback is that there is no diff possible (there may be after dezipping the file, but it would be polluted)...
2. Another gitlab repository converts these XLSX files to raw YAML, and then cleaner YAML files. These are diffable, and modifications can be validated in the XLSX directory with merge requests. 
3. A new XML file is created for each section of the legislation (e.g. _prélèvements sociaux_ or _prestations sociales_), whose content is a merge of both worlds. 
   - it has OpenFisca's param format (`<BAREME>`, `<NODE>`, `<VALUE>`...)
   - names and paths are updated to be clearer.
   - it has _barèmes IPP_ 's hierarchy

OpenFisca's param names are mapped to new names in this [file](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/baremes-ipp/openfisca_france/param/param-to-parameters.yaml)
_Barèmes IPP_ tables are transformed into OpenFisca XML nodes with this huge [script](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/baremes-ipp/openfisca_france/param/ipp_tax_and_benefit_tables_to_parameters.py)
This [script](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/baremes-ipp/openfisca_france/scripts/merge_ipp_tax_and_benefit_tables_with_parameters.py) is run to create the new, splitted, parameter files, e.g. [_prélèvements sociaux_](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/baremes-ipp/openfisca_france/parameters/prelevements_sociaux.xml)
# What remains to do

To complete the first merge :
- Complete the two files above to handle all the tables of _barèmes IPP_. 
- Rewrite formula calls to new parameters

Afterwards, 
- IPP will make changes to XLS files, whose changes will be integrated with the same process
- OpenFisca contributors will change the new parameter files, and use the issue / PR label `parameter` so that IPP can reflect changes in their tables. See #407
- A new version of _legislation explorer_ will make it possible for everyone to contribute in the same place : 
  - signal parameter (or formula) problems directly on the website (relatively easy)
  - browse parameters with a new interface that could replace viewing the XLSX _barèmes IPP_ (relatively easy)
  - edit parameters directly in this interface (forms to edit XML param nodes; should create PRs with github's API)
# Flat params ?

I'm convinced that parameters should be made flat, which would in particular make it easier to develop the editing interface. 
Discussion in #409

Dans OF France coexistent les variables `revenus_locatifs` (variable mensuelle) et `revenus_fonciers_minima_sociaux` (mensuelle aussi, mais découlant des revenus fonciers des déclarations fiscales). Comment gérer ces deux variables, qui traitent des mêmes revenus ?

Rather than having two separate formulas `fnal_tranche_a` (for companies ≤ 20 employees) and `fnal_tranche_a_plus_20` (for companies > 20 employees), it would be much simpler to offer a `fnal_tranche_a` formula that always returns a value, whatever the amount of employees.

issue_title: search method should support start_index and max_results
issue_body:
# Issue
CROWD API search results that exceed the server's maximum result set size are not accessible through the python-crowd.py project. The current search method supports CROWD API query parameter of entity_type for the /usermanagement/1/search resource, but has not implemented support for start_index or max_results parameters. 

# Solution
I propose to enhance the search method to implement two additional query parameters which the CROWD API supports: start_index and max_results. Implementation should include default values of start_index=0 and max_results=99999 to maintain backwards compatibility with existing tools not currently supplying these arguments when calling the search method.

# Benefits
Supporting start_index and max_results query parameters will…

- Allow retrieval of results exceeding the CROWD server's maximum results set size.
- Allow performance tuning if multiple, smaller query results would be better for server or client performance.

# Potential Issues
Existing tools using the python-crowd module that rely upon a server's default max_results setting may encounter a change in behavior as the query results could be larger (or smaller) than anticipated due to the proposed default value of 99999. According to the Atlassian REST API policy, the default and maximum sizes of paged data are not considered part of the API and may change without notice. Tools using the CROWD API should declare and validate results set sizes themselves. [https://developer.atlassian.com/platform/marketplace/atlassian-rest-api-policy/#AtlassianRESTAPIpolicy-Resource/ratelimitsandpaging](https://developer.atlassian.com/platform/marketplace/atlassian-rest-api-policy/#AtlassianRESTAPIpolicy-Resource/ratelimitsandpaging)

from manticore.ethereum import ManticoreEVM

m = ManticoreEVM()

m.verbosity(3)

contract C {

    }

    }
}

owner_account = m.create_account(balance=10000000)

contract_account = m.solidity_create_contract(source_code, owner=owner_account)

print "[+] Hooking 'to_hook' function"

def to_hook(state):

signatures = m.get_metadata(contract_account.address).signatures
signatures = {v: k for k, v in signatures.iteritems()}
m.add_hook(int(signatures["to_hook(uint256)"],16), to_hook)
contract_account.f(None)

issue_title: GWCS transformations lose accuracy near CRVAL and/or CRPIX (or near native poles)
issue_body:
Quite possible this issue may be related to issue #49. First of all, based on my tests, it appears that GWCS has extremely limited accuracy of inverse transformations (and possible forward too). This low accuracy makes GWCS unusable for chained transformations from one WCS to another (=re-projections).

Here is an example of a case when accuracy is lost and I also compare the results from GWCS with results from FITS WCS:

```python
>>> # Define FITS WCS:
>>> from astropy import wcs
>>> fwi = wcs.WCS()
>>> fwi.wcs.pc = [[-1.666667E-05, 0], [0, 1.666667E-05]]
>>> fwi.wcs.cdelt = [1, 1]
>>> fwi.wcs.crpix = [4100.5, -8157.5]
>>> fwi.wcs.crval = [150.1163213, 2.200973097]
>>> fwi.wcs.ctype=['RA---TAN','DEC--TAN']
>>> fwi.wcs.radesys='ICRS'
>>> fwi.wcs.set()
>>> 
>>> # Define "similar" GWCS:
>>> import gwcs
>>> from astropy.modeling.models import (Shift, Scale, RotateNative2Celestial,
...                                      AffineTransformation2D, Rotation2D, Pix2Sky_TAN )
>>> from astropy import coordinates as coord
>>> sky_frame = gwcs.coordinate_frames.CelestialFrame(reference_frame=coord.ICRS(),name='icrs')
>>> transform = (Shift(-fwi.wcs.crpix[0]) & Shift(-fwi.wcs.crpix[1]) |
...              AffineTransformation2D(matrix=fwi.wcs.pc, translation=[0, 0]) |
...              Scale(fwi.wcs.cdelt[0]) & Scale(fwi.wcs.cdelt[1]) |
...              Pix2Sky_TAN() |
...              RotateNative2Celestial(fwi.wcs.crval[0], fwi.wcs.crval[1], 180))
>>> gwi = gwcs.WCS(forward_transform=transform, output_frame=sky_frame)
>>> 
>>> # Check that RA, DEC at CRPIX is identical to CRVAL:
>>> gwi(*fwi.wcs.crpix)
(150.1163213, 2.2009730969999963)
>>> gwi(*fwi.wcs.crpix) - fwi.wcs.crval
array([  0.00000000e+00,  -3.55271368e-15])
>>> 
>>> # Now convert (inverse transform) CRVAL to image coordinates and compare with CRPIX:
>>> gwi.invert(*gwi(*fwi.wcs.crpix)) - fwi.wcs.crpix
array([ 0.        , -0.05122641])
>>> # This is a very large error!!!
>>>
>>> # Compare previous result with the results from the same transformations
>>> # performed with FITS WCS:
>>> fwi.wcs_world2pix(fwi.wcs_pix2world([fwi.wcs.crpix], 1), 1) - fwi.wcs.crpix
array([[  0.00000000e+00,  -2.10093276e-10]])
>>> fwi.wcs_world2pix(fwi.wcs_pix2world([fwi.wcs.crpix], 0), 0) - fwi.wcs.crpix
array([[  6.74845069e-10,  -8.70386430e-10]])
>>> # Notice that FITS WCS is 8 orders of magnitude more accurate than GWCS *regardless* of
>>> # "origin" setting (as it should be)!
```

issue_title: What should be done about non-atomicity of Table.counter_set()
issue_body:
See #1541 for discussion.

There is a [note][1] from the HappyBase source code

> Note that application code should *never* store a incremented or decremented counter value directly; use the atomic `Table.counter_inc` and `Table.counter_dec` methods for that.

and we just went one step further and just made `Table.counter_set()` a not implemented method (it raises `NotImplementedError` when called).

[1]: https://github.com/wbolster/happybase/blob/9cbd718c10a3089f234f1eac1236b631e1f8e7cd/happybase/table.py#L531-L534

For example `gcloud-python/0.3.0-GAE` instead of just ``gcloud-python/0.3.0`.

See discussion in #561 for context.

issue_title: Cloud Spanner Client should prevent nested transactions
issue_body:
Cloud Spanner does not support [nested transactions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nested_transaction). But the client library does allow users to call `database.run_in_transaction` inside of the callback that is provided to an outer `database.run_in_transaction`. This is misleading since users might believe that this will behave like a nested transaction but in reality these will be two independent transactions. This is confusing and a source of bugs. Specifically the inner transaction might succeed while the outer might ABORT, in which case the callback will be rerun thus rerunning the inner transaction. 
We should prevent this from happening by detecting and raising an error if someone calls `database.run_in_transaction` inside the callback provided to `database.run_in_transaction`.  Note that this needs to be done before Beta since this is a known breaking change.
Also note that if we come across a use case where we do want to allow calling a nested `database.run_in_transaction`, we should be able to enable that in future without making a breaking change.

issue_title: Different bytes dumped for python types
issue_body:
In order to maintain the same hashes in joblib's argument hasher when switching to dill, objects which can be pickled by pickle should return the same bytes. This is currently not the case for types themselves, e.g. `ValueError`:

    >>> dill.dumps(ValueError)
    b'\x80\x03cdill.dill\n_load_type\nq\x00X\n\x00\x00\x00ValueErrorq\x01\x85q\x02Rq\x03.'
    >>> pickle.dumps(ValueError)
    b'\x80\x03cbuiltins\nValueError\nq\x00.'

Importing numpy in this common way leads to pickle failures.

    from numpy import *
    import dill
    dill.dump_session()

Specifically:

    pickle.PicklingError: Can't pickle <type 'numpy.uint64'>: it's not the same object as numpy.uint64

and very very probably several others.

This is a bit of a big request to add all of the `numpy` top-level objects, but it's a very common case.

issue_title: DuplicateDetector: broken for jvm_binary that has `source=...`,  does not work for rest.
issue_body:
Reported the following backtrace upgrading from 0.0.51 to 0.0.52 or 0.0.52:
```
File "/root/.cache/pants/setup/bootstrap/pants.zgaZaX/install/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pants/backend/jvm/tasks/detect_duplicates.py", line 119, in _get_conflicts_by_artifacts
    conflicts_by_artifacts[tuple(sorted(artifacts))].add(file_name)
  File "/root/.cache/pants/setup/bootstrap/pants.zgaZaX/install/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pants/build_graph/target.py", line 510, in __lt__
    return self.address < other.address

Exception message: 'unicode' object has no attribute ‘address’ 
```

The `sorted(artifacts)` are a mixture of jar paths for external dependencies and Targets for internal dependencies.  This mixture breaks the Target `__lt__` operator method - its not robust to the mix.  So this is the hard break.  It _should_ be a hard break for ~every JvmBinary target - to have a binary you need a source (internal dep of self), or 1 or more dependencies on internal jvm targets to provide the main the binary runs.  This leads to the soft break - the internal class and resource detection stops at the binary, but should be recursive.

issue_title: Eagerly fetch stdout, stderr, and output files from remote execution responses
issue_body:
Right now when remote execution finishes, we just return whether it was successful, and any inline stdout/stderr.

Instead, we should fetch the stdout and stderr if it's not inline, and populate those fields, and fetch any output files and dump them in the store.

This involves editing [this code](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/628c83d5ab2706b0f64d69568c57a718ec7c5e2a/src/rust/engine/process_execution/src/remote.rs#L219-L223) to:
1. Check whether `stdout_raw` or `stdout` is set, and if the latter, fetch it and populate the value. Same for stderr.
2. Add code to [Store](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/628c83d5ab2706b0f64d69568c57a718ec7c5e2a/src/rust/engine/fs/src/store.rs#L40) to download directories recursively, using the `remote::ByteStore`'s [existing code to download blobs](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/628c83d5ab2706b0f64d69568c57a718ec7c5e2a/src/rust/engine/fs/src/store.rs#L1400-L1446).
3. Add expected output files to [ExecuteProcessRequest](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/628c83d5ab2706b0f64d69568c57a718ec7c5e2a/src/rust/engine/process_execution/src/lib.rs#L27), populate it [on the Action](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/628c83d5ab2706b0f64d69568c57a718ec7c5e2a/src/rust/engine/process_execution/src/remote.rs#L183-L185), and call the fetch code from 2 [when we get a response](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/628c83d5ab2706b0f64d69568c57a718ec7c5e2a/src/rust/engine/process_execution/src/remote.rs#L219-L223)

If I have time later I might finish this with proper support and a PR.

issue_title: Old versions of the daemon are stopped by an incompatible killall command
issue_body:
Per @akavel in #307, killall is not a command in Windows, so the call to killall ipfs via the subprocess module in replay.py will not work on Windows (sans Cygwin).

This is less critical, as a means was introduced in IPFS v0.4.10 to allow the ipfsapi to shutdown the daemon. This is already implemented and in-place but the fall-through if this does not work is to killall, so it might be invoked in some unanticipated circumstances.

I am unaware of a cross-platform single line solution to kill a process by name. To account for Windows, we may try `os.system('ipfs')` if `killall` is not available. If we had a list of all ipfs pids, we could also loop through `os.kill(pid, signal.SIGKILL)`.

issue_title: _stream_helper returns invalid JSON or cannot decode
issue_body:
**docker-py**
1.8.0

**python**
3.5.0

**docker**
Version 1.11.1-beta10 (build: 6662)

**OS**
Mac OS X 10.11 Beta 15A279b

- - -

While using https://github.com/6si/shipwright, I found that invalid JSON was being returned by `docker-py`. According to the docs, the output of `client.build` should be an iterable of valid JSON objects encoded as strings (or the JSON objects decoded if `decode=True` is passed). e.g.

```
'{"stream":"Step 1 : FROM ubuntu:xenial\\n"}'
```

However, I have found while using the setup listed above, that it is returning strings like:

```
'{"stream":"Step 1 : FROM ubuntu:xenial\\n"}\r\n{"stream":"Step 2 : COPY foo\\n"}\r\n'
```

(Note the trailing CRLFs as well, differs from the documentation)

I suspect this issue is being triggered by the Docker side of things, it looks like the chunks in the chunked response used to align exactly with JSON objects, but now no longer do, although I'm not familiar enough with the Docker API to know if this is correct.

I also suspect I reached this issue because of the pre-release Docker installation, however I see that `docker-py` doesn't list compatible Docker versions, which makes it difficult to know if this is a bug or unimplemented functionality.

issue_title: Update website to report training variance
issue_body:
We've known we should do this for a while now - just making an issue for it so it's in a tracker.

We will keep getting people confused about why their training run doesn't match our models' numbers until we have some discussion of this on the website where we report performance numbers.  Even though anyone who's trained a neural net knows how finicky they are, they often don't make the connection that the finickiness is often due just to training variance, and that a reference implementation of someone else's model will _also_ be finicky.  [Here's](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/issues/379#issuecomment-336718417) some text that should be a good starting place for something to put on the website.

issue_title: Switch to go 1.5 for product build / test
issue_body: Improvements in the new Go compiler toolchain suggest significant performance benefits. However, Deis' integration tests use stdout naively and will break if `GOMAXPROCS > 1`. So for the time being we could upgrade CI nodes to Go 1.5 but add `GOMAXPROCS=1` to the CI test jobs.

I found this script https://gist.github.com/hailiang/0f22736320abe6be71ce that could complement the existing checks in go:

1. [gofmt](http://golang.org/cmd/gofmt/)
2. [goimports](https://github.com/bradfitz/goimports)
3. [golint](https://github.com/golang/lint)
4. [go vet](http://golang.org/cmd/vet)
5. [race detector](http://blog.golang.org/race-detector)
6. [test coverage] (http://blog.golang.org/cover)

While not all clusters are made the same (some use broadcast (cloud haskell), some DNS SRV records (eventstore), some have a single bootstrap IP (consul) and some have any bootstrap IP (riak)) -- it would be great to document a way of coordinating the coalescing and start of a cluster on Deis with the aim to let it spawn on its own, automatically (without intervention with `nse`).

Related to #3227, #3072 -- let me know if I've missed an existing issue discussing this.

As soon as globus/docs.globus.org#845 is resolved, we should add this to either `AuthClient` or `ConfidentialAppAuthClient`.
We should wait for the docs to be updated so that we can put a proper `External Documentation` section into the docstring.

@mattias-lidman or @bjmc, can you confirm that this does or does not work for Native App clients?
I would assume that, like introspect, it requires an ID and secret.

If this is the superior call for basic validity checking, we should be sure to update the docstring on introspect to recommend using it instead where possible.

issue_title: activate/conda/deactivate scripts created when they shouldn't be...
issue_body:
I have a package I'm building called Burger (*) sitting in my home directory: `/Users/mcg/burger/`, and given standard Python packaging structure there's also a directory `/Users/mcg/burger/burger/`.

Being the smart person I am I create a separate conda environment for my project called, you guessed it, `burger`. So when I'm ready to develop I do
```
cd ~/burger
source activate burger
```
Every once in awhile my twitchy fingers try to autocomplete that last `burger` and I get this:
```
cd ~/burger
source activate burger/
```
This, of course, doesn't do what I want: it activates a conda environment _that doesn't exist_, in the location `/Users/mcg/burger/burger/`. When things don't work right I realize my mistake and do `source activate burger` and all is well.

Or is it? Alas, that incorrect `activate` command left me a little present: a directory `/Users/mcg/burger/burger/bin` containing symbolic links to `activate`, `deactivate`, and `conda`. 

They just sit there, lurking quietly, but not really doing anything bad... unless I accidentally check them into my repo, in which case they [break my CI builds](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1509).

I would suggest that the script that creates these symbolic links should do at least a _cursory_ check to confirm that the environment it is activating is legitimate. If it is not, it should  issue an error. The consequences of not doing so seem subtle and usually benign, but take my word for it, they're at times quite vexing.

_(*) the name of the actual package has been changed to protect the innocent._

issue_title: Issues from a conda discussion on Hacker News
issue_body:
From here: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=9861234

Agreed (although biased since I used to work at Continuum.) I am wondering what others think of conda?

* We have used Conda for our first python deployment and the process has been seamless. It provides the same sandboxing concept using virtualenvs and also uses prebuilt binaries for native dependencies so you don't have to build them every time. The only drawback I would say is that we have to install miniconda in our production servers rather than just deploying an standalone package.

*  My team has been rolling our own conda packages for (frequent) internal software releases to local servers and have been pretty happy overall pulling down code from a locally managed conda package repo.
With that said, Conda is not a perfect solution. One thing that can be frustrating is that a package can include compiled code (shared objects/dylibs) that may be incompatible with your system. Unfortunately, while you can indicate dependencies on other conda packages, python versions, etc there isn't currently a convenient way to indicate things like GLIBC dependencies.

I messed something up in my dev environment and wanted to remove a package but always get an error. 

```
conda uninstall --force vega                                                                                                                                                                                                                          3779ms
Fetching package metadata .............

PackageNotFoundError: Package(s) is missing from the environment:
            vega
```

How do I delete a package that is not cooperating?
